127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.

19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

2879	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
2880	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
2881	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
2882	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
2883	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
2884	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
2885	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
2886	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
2887	  \(\iota\)).

2889	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
2890	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
2891	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
2892	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
2893	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
2894	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
2895	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
2896	  identification
2897	  \[
2898	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
2899	    \qquad (V \le U),
2900	  \]
2901	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
2902	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
2903	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
2904	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
2905	\end{proof}

2908	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]

2924	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
2925	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2926	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of

2930	\begin{proof}
2931	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
2932	        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
2933	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2934	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
2935	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
2936	  \[
2937	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
2938	  \]
2939	  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\). Its first three steps run exactly
2940	  as for the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, because both
2941	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form
2942	  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a presheaf:
2943	  \begin{enumerate}
2944	    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
2945	      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
2946	      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
2947	    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
2948	      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
2949	      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
2950	      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
2951	    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
2952	      to the presheaf-level comparison.
2953	  \end{enumerate}
2954	  These three steps, together with the open-immersion pushforward comparison \emph{H1},
2955	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of
2956	  two left adjoints of the same pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\)
2957	  together with \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), are reused \emph{verbatim} from
2958	  the tensor and do typecheck for the dual.
2959	
2960	  Here the parallel with the tensor \emph{ends}. After H1 the residual to close is
2961	  \[
2962	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,A)
2963	      \;\cong\;
2964	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr),
2965	  \]
2966	  i.e.\ the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual. For the tensor
2967	  the corresponding residual closed through the ring-iso commutation, because
2968	  \(M \otimes_X N\) is \emph{sectionwise} \(M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\) --- a literal
2969	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image, to which the strong-monoidal comparison applies.
2970	  The dual is \emph{not} of this form: \(\mathtt{dual} = \mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
2971	  is the \emph{slice} internal hom, whose value at an open \(V\) is a module of
2972	  morphisms over the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), not a sectionwise
2973	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image. Consequently the ring-iso/dual commutation
2974	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} is only the \emph{ModuleCat-level shadow}
2975	  of the comparison; it does \emph{not} by itself discharge the residual, and the naive
2976	  ``verbatim mirror'' \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso step does not close it.
2977	
2978	  The residual is the classical presheaf-level comparison: for an open immersion
2979	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual,
2980	  \[
2981	    f_*\mathcal{H}\!om(A, \mathcal{O}) \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}\!om(f_*A, f_*\mathcal{O}).
2982	  \]
2983	  This comparison is constructed objectwise. For each open \(V \subseteq U\), both sides
2984	  compute an \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module of \(\mathcal{O}|_V\)-linear maps; the only
2985	  difference is that the left-hand side carries the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure
2986	  and the right-hand side the \(\mathcal{O}_U(V)\)-module structure, which the sectionwise
2987	  ring isomorphism \(f^\#_V \colon \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_U(V)\)
2988	  identifies canonically. The per-section bridge is exactly
2989	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}; assembling into a presheaf natural
2990	  isomorphism uses only that \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin category, so all naturality
2991	  squares commute automatically. Combining this comparison with the trivialisation gives
2992	  \[
2993	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U

2995	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
2996	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,

2999	  locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
3000	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).

3003	  residual via the fixed-value-category sheaf-site equivalence
3004	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}: that equivalence is parametric in a
3005	  fixed value category and cannot transport the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module
3006	  fibration the presheaf dual carries. The objectwise route above is correct and avoids
3007	  that detour.
3008	\end{proof}

3013	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
3014	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
3015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
3016	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
3017	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
3018	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
3019	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
3020	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
3021	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
3022	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
3023	  isomorphism.
3024	\end{lemma}
3025	
3026	\begin{proof}
3027	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
3028	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
3029	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
3030	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
3031	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
3032	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
3033	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
3034	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
3035	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
3036	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
3037	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
3038	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
3039	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
3040	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
3041	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
3042	  itself is an isomorphism.
3043	\end{proof}

3051	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
3052	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism

3059	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
3060	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\

3073	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that

3081	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
3082	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
3083	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.

3086	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
3087	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
3088	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
3089	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
3090	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
3091	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
3092	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
3093	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
3094	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
3095	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
3096	  strictly more bookkeeping.

3098	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}

3101	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
3102	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
3103	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
3104	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole

3106	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
3107	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
3108	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
3109	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.

3111	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
3112	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
3113	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
3114	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the

3116	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is

3118	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset

3120	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) consumes the \emph{functor-direction} face of the

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Consider the rule
  % $$
  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
  % $$
  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
  (tag area 01CM).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
  \[
    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
  \]
  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
\end{proof}

\section{Internal Hom}
\label{section-internal-hom}

\noindent
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
Consider the rule
$$
U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
$$
It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
\ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
abelian groups. In addition, given an element
$\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
$f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
We will denote this sheaf
$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
$$
\mathcal{F}
\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
\longrightarrow
\mathcal{G}.
$$
For every $x \in X$ there is also a canonical morphism
$$
\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})_x
\to
\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}(\mathcal{F}_x, \mathcal{G}_x)
$$
which is rarely an isomorphism.

\begin{lemma}
[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
  % then the canonical map
  % $$
  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
  % \longrightarrow
  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
  % $$
  % is an isomorphism."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
  \]
  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.

  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
  definitional equality: the two sides are not equal on the nose --- they carry
  \emph{different module structures} that a sectionwise ring isomorphism must reconcile
  --- so the comparison must be constructed and its naturality verified, even though the
  underlying additive groups of functionals coincide. The construction lives at the
  \emph{presheaf-of-modules level}, where the residual genuinely sits, and is carried out
  objectwise in the open \(V\) (with \(V \subseteq U\)) from three ingredients.

  \emph{(a) The per-\(V\) slice equivalence.} Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
  direct-image-of-opens functor \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) is fully faithful with image
  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); consequently, for each open \(V \subseteq U\) there is
  an equivalence of slice categories
  \[
    \mathtt{Over}_Y\,V \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Over}_X\bigl(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\,V\bigr),
  \]
  the per-open shadow of the reindexing equivalence
  \(\mathtt{TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence}\). This identifies the indexing slice
  over which the internal hom on \(Y\) computes its value at \(V\) with the corresponding
  slice over \(X\). (This is the per-open, presheaf-level instrument; it is \emph{not} the
  sheaf-site equivalence \(\mathtt{overSliceSheafEquiv}\) of
  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}, which is parametric in a fixed value
  category and cannot transport the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module fibration the
  dual carries.)

  \emph{(b) Agreement of the codomain.} The codomain of the internal hom on both sides is
  the structure sheaf. On opens \(V \subseteq U\) the structure sheaf of \(Y\) agrees with
  that of \(X\) under the identification of (a) --- \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V) =
  \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\) up to the canonical comparison --- so the target side of the hom
  matches under the slice reindexing.

  \emph{(c) The ring-iso transport of the module structure.} The only remaining
  discrepancy is the module structure. The left-hand side
  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f\) carries, at \(V\), the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure;
  the right-hand side \(\mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\) carries the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module
  structure. The sectionwise comparison
  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathtt{hom}_V \colon
  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}, so
  restriction of scalars along \(\beta_V\) is an \emph{equivalence}
  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isEquivalence\_of\_ringEquiv}\)). Its ModuleCat-level shadow
  on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}: it carries the
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly.

  Composing (a), (b) and (c) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values, and one checks
  naturality in \(V\). The slice-reindexing coherence (a) is \emph{light}, because
  \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin poset and all the relevant squares commute by
  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); but the module-structure transport (c) does
  \emph{not} trivialise --- it is the substantive content, the place where the two sides
  differ by a real ring isomorphism rather than an identity. Assembling the sectionwise
  isomorphisms into a presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism, and sheafifying through the
  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, yields the stated isomorphism
  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
  \]
  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\). Its first three steps run exactly
  as for the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, because both
  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form
  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a presheaf:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
      to the presheaf-level comparison.
  \end{enumerate}
  These three steps, together with the open-immersion pushforward comparison \emph{H1},
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of
  two left adjoints of the same pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\)
  together with \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), are reused \emph{verbatim} from
  the tensor and do typecheck for the dual.

  Here the parallel with the tensor \emph{ends}. After H1 the residual to close is
  \[
    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,A)
      \;\cong\;
    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr),
  \]
  i.e.\ the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual. For the tensor
  the corresponding residual closed through the ring-iso commutation, because
  \(M \otimes_X N\) is \emph{sectionwise} \(M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\) --- a literal
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image, to which the strong-monoidal comparison applies.
  The dual is \emph{not} of this form: \(\mathtt{dual} = \mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
  is the \emph{slice} internal hom, whose value at an open \(V\) is a module of
  morphisms over the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), not a sectionwise
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image. Consequently the ring-iso/dual commutation
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} is only the \emph{ModuleCat-level shadow}
  of the comparison; it does \emph{not} by itself discharge the residual, and the naive
  ``verbatim mirror'' \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso step does not close it.

  The residual is the classical presheaf-level comparison: for an open immersion
  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual,
  \[
    f_*\mathcal{H}\!om(A, \mathcal{O}) \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}\!om(f_*A, f_*\mathcal{O}).
  \]
  This comparison is constructed objectwise. For each open \(V \subseteq U\), both sides
  compute an \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module of \(\mathcal{O}|_V\)-linear maps; the only
  difference is that the left-hand side carries the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure
  and the right-hand side the \(\mathcal{O}_U(V)\)-module structure, which the sectionwise
  ring isomorphism \(f^\#_V \colon \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_U(V)\)
  identifies canonically. The per-section bridge is exactly
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}; assembling into a presheaf natural
  isomorphism uses only that \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin category, so all naturality
  squares commute automatically. Combining this comparison with the trivialisation gives
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
      \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_U\bigr)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
  \]
  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\). Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is
  locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).

  \emph{Route note (iter-230, settled empirically).} Do not attempt to discharge the
  residual via the fixed-value-category sheaf-site equivalence
  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}: that equivalence is parametric in a
  fixed value category and cannot transport the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module
  fibration the presheaf dual carries. The objectwise route above is correct and avoids
  that detour.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
  \]
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\(\mathtt{dual\_unit\_iso}\)):
      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
\end{proof}
